danger_signals.visual_form: Visible monsters of various horror archetypes (zombies, vampires, werewolves, chainsaw maniacs, aliens, giant creatures)
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player and neighbor safety; combat required

danger_signals.visual_form: Neighbor NPC positioned near or surrounded by enemies
danger_signals.meaning: Player must intervene quickly or neighbor will be killed, reducing mission progress

danger_signals.visual_form: Day-to-night progression in certain levels; changing light and sky color
danger_signals.meaning: World state shift: tourists transform into werewolves at nightfall and become unsaveable

danger_signals.visual_form: Player character visual degradation or health bar reduction
danger_signals.meaning: Player approaching game over state if all lives exhausted

danger_signals.visual_form: Visually blocked passages requiring key items
danger_signals.meaning: Area access restricted; progression gated by inventory management

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible items scattered throughout levels (health packs, potions, weapons)
safety_signals.meaning: Resources available to enhance survivability and combat effectiveness

safety_signals.visual_form: Door opening after touching a neighbor or meeting rescue quota per level
safety_signals.meaning: Level progression enabled; forward movement possible

safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple unconventional weapons available (water gun, bazooka, weed-whacker, crucifix, silverware, flamethrower)
safety_signals.meaning: Player has tactical options; different enemies have exploitable weaknesses

safety_signals.visual_form: Neighborhoods, shopping malls, accessible outdoor areas
safety_signals.meaning: Relatively known environments; not inherently hostile locations until monsters arrive

safety_signals.visual_form: Scoring points from defeated enemies and rescued neighbors
safety_signals.meaning: Player resource replenishment; death penalty mitigation

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Teenage protagonists; protagonists armed with improvised/unconventional weapons
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Present in suburban neighborhoods, shopping malls, pyramids, castles; operate across multiple themed level zones

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Horror film archetypes: zombies (shambling undead), vampires (fanged humanoids), werewolves (bestial transformations), chainsaw maniacs (weaponized humans), aliens (UFO occupants), demonic entities
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Regional censorship variants: European versions replace chainsaw maniacs with axe-wielding lumberjacks; blood depicted as purple ooze in Nintendo-censored versions
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle stronghold (Dr. Tongue's base); dispersed across suburban and exotic level settings indicating occupation/invasion

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Various civilian archetypes: barbecue chefs, teachers, babies, tourists, archaeologists, soldiers, dogs, cheerleaders
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Tourists transform visually into werewolves at nightfall in certain levels, becoming enemies rather than rescuees
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Found throughout suburban and exotic zones; represent civilian population under monster occupation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Continuous enemy spawns; monsters visible in all level zones; civilian panic implied through rescue mechanics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies actively hunt and kill unprotected neighbors; player must engage in combat throughout levels

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bright lighting; clear sightlines; open suburban environments with normal aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tourists remain human and rescueable; standard enemy behavior; reduced supernatural threat implied

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sky darkens; lighting shifts to night-time palette in specific levels; visual transition indicating temporal progression
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tourists transform into werewolves (visual metamorphosis); formerly rescueable civilians become hostile; behavioral state shift indicating supernatural influence tied to diurnal cycle

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Haunted castle architecture; distinctive environment separate from suburban zones; visual marker of villain's lair
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final confrontation location; heightened monster concentration; narrative culmination point

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Weapon and power-up distribution varies per level; visible collectibles indicate available tactical options
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player forced to adapt weapon selection to enemy types; resource availability gates combat effectiveness

environmental_storytelling.example: Levels set in pyramids, castles, shopping malls, and suburbs establish genre references and narrative scope: Dr. Tongue's invasion spans multiple locations and cultural settings, emphasizing widespread threat

environmental_storytelling.technique: Enemy archetypes as horror film homage
environmental_storytelling.example: Monsters directly reference 1950s horror films and modern slashers (Friday the 13th, Texas Chain Saw Massacre), establishing game's tone and setting's vulnerability to classic monster threats
Visible neighbors in danger and death animations communicate narrative stakes; each rescueable neighbor represents community under threat; visual reminder of mission criticality

environmental_storytelling.technique: Weapon effectiveness tied to monster lore
environmental_storytelling.example: Silverware kills werewolves in one hit; crucifix effective against vampires; weaponry reflects classic horror-film monster vulnerabilities, embedding world logic in mechanical feedback

Daytime-to-nightfall progression in specific levels with visual sky/lighting change communicates supernatural calendar; tourist-to-werewolf transformation tied to temporal state reinforces horror genre conventions

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Neutral civilians; rescueable by player contact; represent innocent civilians
Transform visually into werewolves; become hostile enemies; no longer rescueable; game counts transformation as civilian death
Weaponized melee combatants; threat to player and neighbors
entity_behavioral_tells.notes: European censored versions replaced with axe-wielding lumberjacks, indicating regional content sensitivity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Huntable enemies; vulnerable to crucifix weapons; reference classic vampire lore
Huntable enemies; instant kill via silverware; reference classic transformation trope
Die instantly if touched by any enemy; become unrescueable until 'Extra Bonus Victim' awarded; visual tell: death animation
Life counter decreases; upon zero lives, game over triggered
Final antagonist; represents ultimate monster threat; narrative climax

notes: Zombies Ate My Neighbors communicates world-state primarily through enemy archetypes, environmental theming, and temporal transitions. The day-to-night mechanic with tourist transformation is a key world-state signal tied to supernatural progression. Faction identity relies on visual monster design referencing specific horror films rather than color/sigil systems. Censorship variants (purple blood, censored enemy types) represent regional world-state interpretation differences. The game uses resource availability (weapons, power-ups, neighbors) as danger/safety signaling rather than explicit UI elements. Neighbor rescue mechanics embed stakes through visual vulnerability.
